one_line_takeaway: A preliminary code-inspection taxonomy (Impact + Oversight, five attributes, three levels) that scores AI agent autonomy statically from orchestration code; piloted on ten AutoGen apps with substantial overall rater agreement (Fleiss' k=0.64) but weak agreement on the Action attribute (k=0.30).
key_finding: Differentiated code-inspection scoring of agent autonomy is tractable and reasonably consistent: overall Fleiss' kappa across all categories was 0.64 (substantial agreement) among three raters over ten applications. Agreement varied by attribute - orchestration 0.67, human-in-the-loop 0.65, environment 0.60 (all substantial) but observability 0.47 and action 0.30 fell below the substantial-agreement threshold. Framework defaults strongly shaped observed behavior: most applications used AutoGen's default human-oversight setups, with Composio a notable exception that consistently overrode defaults to run agent code execution outside a protected Docker container. No application scored 'Lower' on Actions or Observability, which the authors attribute to developers building agents to take actions (not just chat) and to open-source projects favoring logging/transparency.
core_contribution: The paper introduces a code-inspection method for measuring the autonomy of AI agent systems without running them, as an alternative/complement to run-time (behavioral) evaluations. Its own contribution is a two-dimension taxonomy of agent autonomy - Impact (attributes: Actions, Environment) and Oversight (attributes: Orchestration, Human-in-the-loop, Observability) - with each of the five attributes rated at three levels (Lower / Middle / Higher). The taxonomy is operationalized specifically against the AutoGen framework (its human_input_mode, max_rounds/max_consecutive_auto_reply, Docker vs local execution, logging), and validated in a pilot that scores ten real AutoGen applications. The authors state three explicit contributions: (1) a review of how autonomy levels are operationalized in policy and AI, (2) the AutoGen-based autonomy taxonomy, and (3) the pilot code inspection rating ten applications. Note: the cross-domain autonomy-levels table (SAE driving levels, medical robots, aviation, METR task-time levels) is a survey of prior work in Section 2 / Appendix A.1, NOT the paper's own scheme - the paper's own instrument is the AutoGen-focused Table 2.
threat_model: No adversarial threat model is proposed. This is a governance / risk-measurement paper, not an attack-or-defense security paper. It does not model an attacker, exploit, or defense. Its risk framing is about the consequences of autonomous agent behavior (harms drawing policy scrutiny) and it notes downstream security implications - e.g., locally deployed agents with unrestricted code execution 'introducing computer security risks,' and that autonomy assessment can inform 'what level of system security might be important before running' an agent - but these are motivations for governance, not a security threat analysis. Forcing an adversarial security frame onto this paper would misrepresent it.
limitations: Explicitly acknowledged: (1) very small sample - only ten applications - limiting generalizability; (2) code inspection cannot capture emergent, context-specific, or user-interaction-driven behavior, latent runtime dependencies, or environmental feedback loops that run-time evaluation would reveal; (3) low inter-rater agreement on Action (k=0.30) because actions can be enabled by downstream custom tools or additional frameworks not visible in AutoGen config, and on Observability (k=0.47) because distinguishing developer-only logs from meaningful user-facing observability is hard; (4) constrained-environment scoring is unreliable when wider access is possible via API calls to other systems; (5) the human-in-the-loop level is conceptually ambiguous (approval after many tool calls vs approval after each action are hard to rank). The scheme is a preliminary three-level pilot and the authors themselves flag it needs scaling and refinement (e.g., AI-assisted grading, added attributes like goal-setting, inter-agent interaction, and fallbacks).

datasets_used: Ten open-source AutoGen applications (top-5 dependent repos by GitHub stars plus five significant repos): AutoGen Studio, Composio, Sibyl System, Dream Team, GraphRag Ollama, AutoTx, Letta, h2oGPT, Langflow, GPT-Academic
The AutoGen framework itself (source code and default configurations) as the reference for the taxonomy
quantitative_results: Overall inter-rater reliability across all categories: Fleiss' kappa = 0.64 (substantial agreement), 3 raters, 10 applications

Per-attribute Fleiss' kappa: orchestration = 0.67, human-in-the-loop = 0.65, environment = 0.60, observability = 0.47, action = 0.30 (observability and action below the substantial-agreement threshold)
AutoGen repository popularity cited as context: >35,000 stars, 5,100 forks, >2,300 dependents on GitHub
No application scored 'Lower' on the Actions attribute or the Observability attribute (Table 3)
Taxonomy structure: 2 dimensions (Impact, Oversight), 5 attributes, 3 autonomy levels each
baselines_compared: No quantitative baseline comparison is performed; the paper contrasts code-based evaluation against run-time/behavioral evaluation conceptually (Table 1)

Prior autonomy-level schemes are surveyed as related work, not benchmarked against: SAE autonomous-driving levels, medical-robot autonomy (Yang et al.), aviation autonomy, human-oversight levels (Simmler & Frischknecht: in/on/off/out-of-the-loop), LangChain action-space levels, and METR task-time-based autonomy levels (Appendix A.1 / Table 4)
